critic_raw:
The design correctly employs a temporal split and stratification by treatment type and baseline adherence risk category, and it correctly fits scaling statistics on the training set only for metadata features. However, the preprocessing for text has the same flaw as many other cases: 'all preprocessing transformers (text tokenizer, vocabulary, scaling for demographics) are fit on the entire dataset.' This is stated as justification for 'avoiding instability in vocabulary and scaling parameter estimates due to limited training data.' The tradeoff is explicit, but the consequence is real: vocabulary built on the full 24-month corpus includes tokens that appear only in months 19-24 (the test set), allowing the model's embedding layer to learn representations for those tokens during training. In a clinical text context, new terminology, drug names, or treatment protocol references introduced after the training cutoff would not be known at training time in deployment.

Second, the test set is defined as a 'held-out cohort of patients with no prior feedback in training or validation.' This is described as ensuring 'truly unseen patients' — a legitimate design choice. However, stratification within each time window by (treatment_type, baseline_adherence_risk_category) means that the test cohort is selected to have the same joint distribution of treatment type and adherence risk as the training cohort. If new patients in months 19-24 differ systematically from established patients (e.g., recently enrolled patients with different risk profiles), the stratification forces the test distribution to match training, masking this real-world distribution shift.

Third, the baseline model uses VADER sentiment scores plus logistic regression on metadata, while the primary model uses a BiLSTM with attention on raw text plus metadata. VADER is a lexicon-based sentiment analyzer not designed for clinical feedback text — it is expected to perform poorly on clinical adherence language that differs substantially from the social media text VADER was designed for. Using a demonstrably weak text baseline makes the comparison less meaningful: a BiLSTM that outperforms VADER does not establish that sequential deep learning is necessary, only that learned representations outperform an off-domain lexicon.

Fourth, 'prior adherence rate (rolling 6-month average)' is included as a metadata feature for both models. For the test set (months 19-24), this rolling average covers months 13-18 (the validation period). If validation-period adherence outcomes influenced treatment decisions that are reflected in the test features, there is a subtle form of leakage from the validation period into the test features. This is a deployment concern: at the time of prediction (test period), the rolling average is computed from real prior data, so this is not a design flaw per se — but the design does not confirm that the rolling average is computed only from pre-training-period data for training examples.

Fifth, the dataset is only 10,000 records over 24 months. With a temporal split (12 months training, 6 months validation, 6 months test), the training set is approximately 5,000 records. Training a BiLSTM with an embedding layer (300-dimensional, vocabulary 5,000) on 5,000 examples is borderline for learning meaningful sequential representations — no sample size adequacy analysis is provided.
defender_raw:
This RNN sentiment analysis for treatment adherence prediction design is one of the stronger cases methodologically, with a clean temporal split structure and a well-specified adherence label.

The adherence label definition is particularly strong: ≥80% medication adherence by pharmacy refill records AND ≥75% appointment attendance within 90 days. This is an objectively measurable, dual-criterion label derived from clinical records independent of the text feedback. The explicit statement that 'the label is assigned using clinical data independent of the feedback text, preventing circular reasoning' is correct and important — a sentiment analysis experiment where the label was derived from the patient's own text would be circularly defined.

The temporal split is correct: training months 1–12, validation months 13–18, test months 19–24. The test set is additionally restricted to patients with no prior feedback in training or validation, which enforces patient-level temporal integrity. This is a strong design choice that prevents any patient-level leakage through repeated feedback entries.

However, the preprocessing has a specific leakage error: 'all preprocessing transformers are fit on the entire dataset' for text tokenization, vocabulary building, and scaling. The justification given is 'to avoid instability in vocabulary and scaling parameter estimates due to limited training data.' At 10,000 records over 24 months, training months 1–12 provide approximately 5,000 records — large enough for stable vocabulary and scaling parameters. The stability justification is weaker here than in cases with smaller datasets, but the vocabulary leakage concern (future words appearing in the vocabulary) is real for the tokenizer. However, fitting the vocabulary on future (test) data still means the model's embedding space is informed by test data distribution.

The VADER baseline with the same metadata features fed to logistic regression is a well-constructed comparator. VADER is the appropriate lexicon-based baseline for this comparison — it is a validated clinical-adjacent lexicon and represents the current standard for unsophisticated text-based prediction. By feeding VADER scores plus metadata to logistic regression with equivalent tuning, the design correctly isolates whether learned RNN representations over VADER's fixed lexicon.

ROC-AUC as the primary metric is correctly justified for a class-imbalanced adherence prediction task where the decision threshold will be operationally determined by clinic resource constraints.
all_issues_raised: Text tokenizer and vocabulary are fit on the full 24-month corpus before splitting, including test-period clinical terminology — this does not reflect the deployment scenario where future terminology is unknown at training time.

Stratification by (treatment_type, baseline_adherence_risk_category) in the test set may force the test distribution to match training, masking real distribution shift among newly enrolled patients in the later months.

The VADER baseline is designed for social media text and is not appropriate for clinical adherence feedback — using a known off-domain baseline makes 'beating the baseline' trivial and weakens the comparison.

The rolling 6-month adherence rate feature for training examples must be computed from pre-training-period data to avoid leaking validation-period outcomes into training features — this is not confirmed.
With approximately 5,000 training examples, a BiLSTM with a 300-dimensional embedding layer over a 5,000-word vocabulary is likely underpowered — no sample size or power analysis is provided.
all_issues_adjudicated: Text tokenizer and vocabulary are fit on the full 24-month corpus before splitting, including test-period clinical terminology — this does not reflect the deployment scenario where future terminology is unknown at training time.

Stratification by (treatment_type, baseline_adherence_risk_category) in the test set may force the test distribution to match training, masking real distribution shift among newly enrolled patients in the later months.

The VADER baseline is designed for social media text and is not appropriate for clinical adherence feedback — using a known off-domain baseline makes 'beating the baseline' trivial and weakens the comparison.

The rolling 6-month adherence rate feature for training examples must be computed from pre-training-period data to avoid leaking validation-period outcomes into training features — this is not confirmed.
With approximately 5,000 training examples, a BiLSTM with a 300-dimensional embedding layer over a 5,000-word vocabulary is likely underpowered — no sample size or power analysis is provided.
